metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` to the Scope and include a line updating the `explain_criterion` docstring (lines 60-61) to enumerate `commit-trailer` alongside `plan` and `review`.
evidence: Plan Scope lists: registry.py, _guides/commit-ticket-trailer.md, passing-code-review.md, writing-a-passing-plan.md, two test files, AGENTS.md, docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md — _mcp_reads.py is absent.
Plan Approach states: 'served offline by `rebar explain commit-trailer` and the MCP `explain_guide` tool, exactly like `plan`/`review`'.
_mcp_reads.py line 60-61: '``plan`` = how to write a passing plan; ``review`` = how to pass code review' — static string, not dynamically derived from AUTHOR_GUIDES.
MCP `explain_criterion` dispatch at line 67 uses `registry.AUTHOR_GUIDES` (auto-updates), but the docstring is a hardcoded string that doesn't.
findings.finding: The MCP tool docstring in `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` (lines 60-61) hardcodes the guide list as '``plan`` = how to write a passing plan; ``review`` = how to pass code review' — adding `commit-trailer` to `AUTHOR_GUIDES` is not reflected there, and `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` is absent from the plan's Scope section. The plan asserts the MCP `explain_guide` tool will serve the new guide, but the docstring visible to MCP clients will silently omit `commit-trailer`.
scenarios: An MCP client reads the tool's description to discover available guide names — it sees only 'plan' and 'review', never learns about 'commit-trailer', and the feature goal of CLI-discoverability is only half-achieved.
A code-review check flags the stale docstring as a docs drift finding.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_mcp_reads.py`, change the docstring to derive the guide list dynamically (e.g. reference `AUTHOR_GUIDES` keys) or update the static string to include `'commit-trailer' = how to format the commit-ticket trailer`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` to the Scope and specify that the `explain_criterion` tool docstring must be updated to include `commit-trailer` in its enumerated guide-name vocabulary (or refactored to derive the list dynamically from `AUTHOR_GUIDES`).
evidence: MCP docstring (line 59-60): 'OR print an author-facing prose guide when criterion_id is a guide name ("plan" = how to write a passing plan; "review" = how to pass code review)'

Plan Scope section enumerates: registry.py, commit-ticket-trailer.md, passing-code-review.md, writing-a-passing-plan.md, test files, AGENTS.md, docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md — no mention of src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py
CLI _explain builds guide list dynamically: `guides = ', '.join(sorted(registry.AUTHOR_GUIDES))` (line 393) — self-updating, no gap there
MCP docstring is a hardcoded string literal, not derived from AUTHOR_GUIDES at registration time
findings.finding: The MCP `explain_criterion` tool docstring hardcodes the valid guide-name vocabulary (`"plan" = …; "review" = …`) — the instruction an MCP client/LLM reads to discover which `criterion_id` values name guides. After `commit-trailer` is added to `AUTHOR_GUIDES`, this docstring is stale and an MCP consumer following only the tool schema will never discover the new topic. The plan's Scope lists `registry.py`, guide files, and test files but never mentions `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py`.
findings.location: src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py lines 57-63 (explain_criterion tool docstring); Plan § Scope (omits _mcp_reads.py)
scenarios: An MCP client (LLM agent) reads the tool description to discover valid `criterion_id` values for guides, sees only `plan` and `review` listed, and never calls `explain_criterion('commit-trailer')` — defeating the plan's stated goal of making the trailer format discoverable via the MCP explain_guide tool.
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py`, replace the hardcoded `("plan" = …; "review" = …)` in the `explain_criterion` docstring with a dynamically-constructed sentence, e.g. build it at registration time from `registry.AUTHOR_GUIDES`, so adding a new guide to `AUTHOR_GUIDES` automatically updates the tool description without a separate file change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the link-resolution guard's allowed-form set across Approach, Testing, and AC #4 into one canonical list, and add an explicit AC item for the prose `rebar explain <topic>` name-validation sub-check.
evidence: Approach: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`'

Testing: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`.  Any consumer-relative path … FAILS. It also validates every `rebar explain <topic>` mentioned in that prose names a real registered guide/criterion'
AC #4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)' — omits `http://` and the prose `rebar explain` sub-check entirely
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard's allowed-form set is defined inconsistently across three plan sections, leaving the implementer with genuine ambiguity about which set to code the test against. Approach says `https://`/`http://` (off-repo) plus `#anchor`; Testing says `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), `#anchor`, and `mailto:`; AC #4 says only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:` — omitting `http://`. Additionally, the Testing section's prose-mention sub-check ('validates every `rebar explain <topic>` cited in prose names a real registered guide/criterion') has no corresponding AC item, so an implementer can satisfy all ACs while omitting that half of the guard.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) and AC #4
scenarios: An implementer writing the guard test codes it to exactly match AC #4 (the most authoritative section) and omits `http://` from the whitelist — a future guide linking to a non-HTTPS reference fails CI unexpectedly.

An implementer omits the prose `rebar explain <topic>` validation sub-check (not in AC) — a broken `rebar explain nonexistent-topic` reference in a guide body goes undetected, defeating the 'can't rot' claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one canonical allow-list (suggest: `https://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:` for markdown link targets — no `http://` since all canonical links should be HTTPS) and state it once in Testing; promote the prose `rebar explain <topic>` validation to its own AC bullet (e.g., 'every `rebar explain <topic>` cited in any guide body names a real registered guide or criterion id (test passes)').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the allowed-link-target forms for the guard test across Approach, Testing, and AC4: decide definitively whether `rebar explain <topic>` targets (as markdown links) are allowed (and add them to AC4) or whether guide prose uses `rebar explain` only as non-linked text (and clarify the Approach); also confirm whether `mailto:` belongs in the allowed set and add it to the Approach section if so.
evidence: Approach: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `rebar explain` are allowed'
Testing: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`'
AC criterion 4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)' — omits `rebar explain`, omits `http://`
findings.finding: The set of 'allowed' markdown-link target forms in the link-resolution guard is defined differently in three places: Approach says `https://`/`http://`/`#anchor`/`rebar explain`; Testing section says `https://`/`http://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`; AC criterion 4 says `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`. The Approach includes `rebar explain` as a first-class allowed link form; Testing and AC omit it and include `mailto:` instead. An implementer who follows the AC (the authoritative spec) would build a guard that rejects any markdown link whose target starts with `rebar explain` — but the Approach explicitly endorses that form. Separately, `mailto:` appears only in Testing/AC, never in Approach, with no rationale.
findings.location: Approach section (link-resolution guard description) vs. Testing section (guard allowed-forms list) vs. AC criterion 4
scenarios: Implementer builds guard per AC4, which rejects `](rebar explain ...)` links; but Scope says L8/L35 are repointed to `rebar explain <topic>` — if those appear as markdown link targets, the guard fails on valid content.
Implementer builds guard per Approach, which has no `mailto:` allowance; a future `mailto:` link in a guide would be incorrectly flagged.
findings.suggested_fix: If `rebar explain <topic>` references in guides are always plain prose (never `](rebar explain ...)`), state that explicitly in the Approach and remove it from the allowed-link forms. Then align AC4 to read: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://`, `#anchor`, or `mailto:`' — matching the Testing section. If `rebar explain` CAN appear as a markdown link target, add it to both Testing and AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the allowed-link allowlist between the Testing paragraph and AC #4 — state the canonical set once (suggest: `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:`) and reference it in both places.
evidence: Testing: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`'
AC #4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)'
http:// is present in Testing but absent in AC #4
findings.finding: The Testing section allows `http://` links in the guard ('`https://`/`http://` (off-repo)') while AC #4 lists only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`. An agent implementing the test cannot know which allowlist to code without resolving this discrepancy.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC #4
scenarios: Agent codes the guard with `https://` only, causing false failures on any `http://` link in prose.
Agent codes with both, then AC #4's grep/manual check disagrees.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC #4 replace 'only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`' with 'only `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, or `mailto:`' to match the Testing paragraph.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend an existing one) to cover the `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` update — e.g., 'the file notes `rebar explain commit-trailer` and `rebar verify-commit-ticket --help` as discovery paths (verified by grep)'.
evidence: Scope lists: '`docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` — note the CLI discovery paths.'
No AC references `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` or verifies its content.
findings.finding: The scope item `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md — note the CLI discovery paths` has no corresponding acceptance criterion; no AC verifies that this file is updated or what it must contain.
findings.location: Scope section (`docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` line) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all 7 ACs)
scenarios: An implementer skips updating `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` and all ACs still pass; the scope item becomes a dead letter with no enforcement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: '`docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` notes `rebar explain commit-trailer` as the CLI discovery path (grep-verified).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Testing section, clarify the disposition of the existing `test_expected_format_documented_in_reference` test when the new packaged-guide drift test is added (keep both as complementary guards, or update the existing test to also verify the packaged guide path).
evidence: Existing test: `doc = Path(rebar.__file__).parents[2] / 'docs' / 'commit-ticket-trailer.md'` — repo-path-based, fails in a repo-less install.
Plan Testing section: 'Drift: the packaged guide contains `EXPECTED_FORMAT` verbatim.' — new test for packaged guide.
Plan does not mention the existing drift test or whether it is updated/removed/supplemented.
docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md exists and already quotes EXPECTED_FORMAT (confirmed by read).
findings.finding: The existing drift test `test_expected_format_documented_in_reference` (tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py:233-236) checks `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` by constructing a repo-relative path (`Path(rebar.__file__).parents[2] / 'docs' / 'commit-ticket-trailer.md'`). The plan adds a new drift test asserting the PACKAGED guide quotes `EXPECTED_FORMAT`, but does not mention updating or superseding the existing repo-path-based drift test. These two tests can produce contradictory drift signals if the packaged guide and `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` diverge.
scenarios: The existing test silently becomes redundant with the new one, or worse, tests a different file than the one the plan treats as authoritative.

If `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` is updated (per plan Scope) and the packaged guide is also updated, a later edit to one but not the other is caught by only one of the two drift tests — ambiguity about which is the single source of truth for the CLI-served content.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py` to Scope and update `test_wheel_ships_author_guides` to also assert `rebar/_guides/commit-ticket-trailer.md` ships in the wheel (either extend the hardcoded list or switch to iterating `AUTHOR_GUIDES.values()` dynamically).
evidence: tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py line 138: `for guide in ("writing-a-passing-plan.md", "passing-code-review.md"):`
Plan Scope section does not list tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py
Plan Testing section does not mention the wheel-ships-guides test
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py::test_wheel_ships_author_guides hardcodes the two current guide filenames ('writing-a-passing-plan.md', 'passing-code-review.md') and will NOT cover the new 'commit-ticket-trailer.md' guide unless updated. The plan does not list this file in Scope or Testing.
scenarios: The new guide is added to AUTHOR_GUIDES and ships in the wheel, but test_wheel_ships_author_guides never asserts it's present — a future regression (guide accidentally dropped from packaging) goes undetected.
Alternatively, if the test is NOT updated and someone runs the existing test suite, it still passes (hardcoded list only checks the two old guides), giving false confidence.
findings.suggested_fix: In test_wheel_ships_author_guides, replace the hardcoded tuple with `registry.AUTHOR_GUIDES.values()` (importing registry) so any future guide addition is automatically covered, or add `"commit-ticket-trailer.md"` to the existing tuple.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a scope bullet explicitly auditing `explain_criterion(<id>)` renderings and fixing any consumer-relative links found, or (b) narrow the guard test to cover only `AUTHOR_GUIDES` guide bodies (where all fixes are scoped), with a rationale that criteria prose was already verified clean by a prior ticket.
evidence: Scope lists only three guide files for link-rewriting: `commit-ticket-trailer.md` (new), `passing-code-review.md`, `writing-a-passing-plan.md`.
Testing: guard enumerates 'every registered AUTHOR_GUIDES guide body AND every `explain_criterion(<id>)` rendering'
AC #4: 'across every AUTHOR_GUIDES guide body and every `explain_criterion(<id>)` rendering, no markdown link target is consumer-relative'
No scope bullet addresses auditing or fixing consumer-relative links in criterion renderings
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard (AC #4) tests `explain_criterion(<id>)` renderings for consumer-relative links, but the remediation scope only rewrites the three `_guides/*.md` files. If any existing criterion rendering contains a consumer-relative path, the guard will fail and no scoped step exists to fix it — creating an unreachable end-state.
findings.location: Scope section; Testing section (link-resolution guard); Acceptance Criteria AC #4
scenarios: An agent writes the guard, discovers a criterion rendering has a relative link, has no scoped work item to fix it, and cannot make all tests green without unscoped changes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
